segments.s515-g57: دل شهریار جهان is a genitive phrase meaning “the heart of the king of the world” (the ezāfe is understood though not written). شاد باد is an optative clause: شاد is a predicate adjective “happy/glad” and باد is the poetic optative particle “may (it be)”, so the whole segment reads “May the heart of the world’s sovereign be glad.”
segments.s515-g58: ز هر بد is a prepositional phrase “from every evil/ill” (ز = poetic variant of از). تن پاکش = تن + پاک + -ش, where پاک (adjective “pure/clean”) carries the pronominal suffix -ش “his,” so “his body (pure).” آزاد باد again uses باد as optative “may (be) free,” so the clause means “May his (pure) body be free from every evil.” Note the adjective+possessive suffix attaches after the noun rather than with a separate ezāfe.
segments.s515-g41: چنین is a fronted demonstrative/adverb meaning “thus/so,” followed by the copula است (“is”). رسم سرای کهن is a noun phrase ‘the custom/practice of the old house/household/minstrel‑house’; کهن is the adjective ‘ancient/old’ placed after the head noun. The whole segment is a copular clause meaning “thus is the custom of the old house.”
segments.s515-g42: This is two coordinated clauses: سکندر شد uses شد (an historic/habitual past/inchoative) for ‘appeared/arrived/happened (Alexander)’, then و joins the next clause. ماند is a past tense verb ‘remained’ and ایدر is the locative adverb ‘here’ placed before the noun it localizes; سخن (‘speech/tale/word’) is the remaining subject/thing that ‘remained’. Literally: ‘Alexander appeared and the speech remained here.’
segments.s515-g43: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’; او is the subject ‘he’. سی و شش is the numeral ‘thirty‑six’ (و here links the tens and units). پادشا را uses the object marker را to mark the definite object ‘the kings’. بکشت is an archaic perfect/past form meaning ‘slew/killed’ (classical past of کشتن).
segments.s515-g44: نگر is an imperative ‘look!/see!/consider!’. تا here functions in an evaluative sense (‘to see how…’ / ‘as far as’) linking to چه دارد ‘what he has/possesses’. ز گیتی is a genitive‑like phrase ‘of/from the world’, and به مشت is an idiomatic locative/instrumental phrase ‘in (his) fist’ (meaning ‘under his control’). The clause asks the hearer to observe how much of the world he holds.
segments.s515-g45: برآورد is a classical perfective verb here meaning ‘brought forth/established/founded’. پرمایه is an adjective ‘rich/substantial’ placed before the noun; the numeral ده (‘ten’) is embedded with شارستان to give ‘ten prosperous towns/cities’ (the numeral appears in the original segment though not listed among the words here). The whole phrase means he established ten flourishing cities.
segments.s515-g46: شد is the copular/transformative past ‘became’. آن شارستانها is the demonstrative + plural noun ‘those cities’, and کنون is the temporal adverb ‘now’ placed before the predicate. خارستان is the predicate noun ‘thorn‑land/wasteland’. Together: ‘Those cities have now become wastelands.’
segments.s515-g47: بجست is an archaic past form ‘sought’ (classical verb), and آنچ is a relative/indefinite ‘that which/what’. هرگز is the negative‑frequency adverb ‘never’, and نجستست is an archaic perfective form used here in the negative context to mean ‘was not sought/ever sought’ (classical word‑order places کس ‘anyone/no one’ after the verb). The clause reads ‘he sought that which no one had ever sought.’
segments.s515-g48: سخن is the noun ‘word/speech/poem’ and functions as the item that ‘remained’; ماند is the past verb ‘remained’. ازو is the source/genitive ‘from him’, اندر آفاق is the locative phrase ‘within/across the horizons’, and بس is a final particle meaning ‘enough/that is all’. Grammatically this says ‘his speech remained from him across the horizons, and that is enough.’
segments.s515-g49: سخن به که ویران نگردد سخن — The initial سخن ('poem/speech') is topicalized for emphasis; به که here forms the idiom 'it is better that...'. ویران نگردد uses نگردد (گرد + negative prefix ن-) in the present/subjunctive/passive sense 'become ruined / be ruined' (i.e. 'should not be ruined'). The repeated سخن at the end is rhetorical repetition (restating the subject) rather than a new grammatical subject.
segments.s515-g50: چو از برف و باران سرای کهن — چو functions as a comparative 'like/as', introducing a simile. از before برف و باران marks origin/cause ('from / exposed to snow and rain'). سرای کهن is an adjectival phrase 'old house/abode'; in this usage the clause likens the poem to an ancient house that withstands snow and rain.
segments.s515-g51: گذشتم ازین سد اسکندری — گذشتم is the 1st-person past perfect 'I passed/ I have passed'. ازین = از + این ('from this'); the written ین is the bound form of 'this' (poetic/older spelling). سد اسکندری is a genitival phrase 'the Alexandrian/Iskandar barrier' (سد = dam/barrier; اسکندری = 'of Alexander' or 'Alexandrian').
segments.s515-g52: همه بهتری باد و نیک‌اختری — همه 'all/everything' is the subject; بهتری here is a comparative/adjectival predicate 'better' and the following باد is the optative particle 'may (it be)'. The clause is a wish: 'may all be better', and the final نیک‌اختری is an epithet or name in apposition, read as 'and may Nīk-Akhtar be (auspicious/fortunate)'.
segments.s515-g53: اگر چند هم بگذرد روزگار — اگر introduces a conditional 'if/though'. چند هم together mean 'even if a little / however much' (here 'even if (some) time passes'); بگذرد is the subjunctive/present form of گذشتن 'to pass' and روزگار is the subject 'time / the times'. The clause expresses concession: 'even if time passes'.
segments.s515-g54: نوشته بماند ز ما یادگار — نوشته ('the writing' / 'what is written') is the noun subject, and بماند is an optative/subjunctive 'may remain' or simple future sense 'will remain'. ز ما = 'of/from us' marks provenance; یادگار = 'memento/memorial'. Together: 'the writing will remain as a memorial of us.'
segments.s515-g55: اگر صد بمانی و‌گر صدهزار — اگر introduces the conditional 'if'. صد بمانی uses بمانی (subjunctive/potential of ماندن) 'you remain / you should remain' with صد 'a hundred (years implied)'. و‌گر = و + گر ('and if / or if') introduces the alternative 'or even a hundred thousand'. This is a hypothetical concession about long life/long duration.
segments.s515-g56: به خاک اندر آید سرانجام کار — به خاک اندر آید literally 'into the dust comes' where به + خاک = 'into dust', اندر آید = 'comes into / returns to' (آید is present stem 'come'). سرانجام کار 'in the end (سرانجام), the affair/deed (کار)' — idiomatically: 'in the end your deeds/affairs come to dust' (i.e. everything ends in dust).
segments.s515-g25: درختی is an indefinite noun (tree) with the Persian adjectival/indefinite suffix -ی. که introduces a relative/temporal clause here ('that / when'); کشتی is the clause subject ('ship'); چو آمد is a temporal phrase where چو = 'when' and آمد = 'came'. به بار (literally 'to/with load') completes آمد as a verbal phrase meaning 'came bearing a load / became laden'. Word order is noun — relative marker — subject — temporal particle — verb — complement (poetic inversion allowed).
segments.s515-g26: This clause states the speaker's perception: دل خاک is a genitive/possessed phrase 'the heart of the earth' in poetic juxtaposition (no ezâfe particle). بینم is first‑person singular present/subjunctive form of دیدن used as a main verb 'I see' / 'I behold'. ترا is the object 'you' expressed by archaic/poetic enclitic ت plus the object marker را (ثبت emphasis/object). غمگسار is a noun in apposition/vocative modifying ترا: 'consoler'.
segments.s515-g27: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. تاج سپهر is a genitive phrase 'the crown of the heavens' (noun + modifier). اندر آمد is a two‑word verbal expression meaning 'came down / descended into' (اندر = 'into/within', آمد = 'came'). به زیر ('to/under beneath') gives the direction/placement 'beneath'. The clause is a poetic, slightly inverted order but follows subject–verb complements.
segments.s515-g28: بزرگان is the plural subject 'the great ones'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' and governs گفتار 'speech/words'. گشتند is the past 3rd‑person plural of شدن 'became' and predicates on بزرگان. سیر is a predicative adjective 'sated / satisfied'. The sequence means 'the great ones became sated from (their) speech'.
segments.s515-g29: نهفتند is the past tense, 3rd‑person plural transitive verb 'they hid / they buried'. صندوق او را is the object NP 'his chest' with the possessor after the noun (poetic order) and the object marker را attached to the whole NP. به خاک is a prepositional phrase 'into the earth' indicating location. Overall: 'They hid/buried his chest in the earth.'
segments.s515-g30: ندارد is a present tense negative verb 'does not have / there is not' (3rd singular). جهان is the grammatical subject 'the world' (poetic inversion with verb preceding subject). از چنین ترس و باک is a prepositional phrase 'of/from such fear and dread' specifying what the world lacks. Such subject–verb inversion and the linking preposition از are common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s515-g31: ز باد is the agentive/genitive phrase 'by/from the wind'. اندر آرد برد contains two verbs in close sequence (آرد = 'brings/raises', برد = 'takes/carries away') sharing an implicit subject/object in poetic compression — common in Ferdowsi. سوی دم is a directional phrase 'toward the breath / toward the moment' (دم here is temporal: 'a moment' or 'an instant'). The whole clause describes the wind lifting and carrying something away in an instant.
segments.s515-g32: نه ... نه is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor'. دادست is an archaic/past‑stative expression meaning roughly 'there was justice / justice existed' (here negated). پیدا is a predicative adjective 'apparent / found / visible'; it appears twice to parallel the two polarities. ستم is the noun 'oppression / injustice' and is the second member of the 'neither ... nor' pair. The clause therefore says that neither justice nor oppression was manifest.
segments.s515-g9: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that/thereupon' (ازان + پس). بشد is the past/resultative 'it became/it came to be' with روشنک as its subject (subject is proper name Roshanak). پر ز درد is a fixed adjective phrase 'full of pain/grief' (پر = 'full (of)', ز = poetic 'from/of', درد = 'pain/sorrow'), describing Roshanak's state.
segments.s515-g10: چنین گفت is an inverted, compact reporting clause 'thus (she) said' where چنین ('thus') precedes the verb گفت ('said'); the subject (Roshanak) is omitted because it is clear from context. کای شاه آزادمرد is a vocative: کای = 'O', followed by the addressee 'شاه' and the epithet 'آزادمرد' ('noble/generous man'), addressing the king directly.
segments.s515-g11: This is a verbless question with the copula at the end: جهاندار ('sovereign') is the noun, دارای دارا is a genitive/possessive/epithet construction meaning 'possessor/holder of Darā' or 'having Darā', and کجاست is the interrogative copula 'where is (he)'. Word order is [noun + epithet] + question word.
segments.s515-g12: کزو 'from whom' is a relative/ablative pronoun referring back to that sovereign. داشت is past 'had' (possession/held). گیتی 'the world' is the object. همی here is an older continuous/emphatic particle ('ever/continually/indeed') modifying the clause. پشت راست is a compound: پشت = 'support/protection' and راست qualifying it ('upright/firm' or 'true'), so the clause literally says 'from whom the world had its firm/upright support.'
segments.s515-g13: همان at the start is a demonstrative/identifying particle 'that same'. A list follows linked by و ('and'): خسرو, اشک, فریان — here these function as names/epithets or elements in a list of illustrious figures/qualities. The final فور is part of the same enumeration (the exact semantic value is poetic/epic: could be a name or an epithet).
segments.s515-g14: همان here repeats 'that same' and names the famed person: نامور is an epithet 'renowned/famed' modifying خسرو, followed by شهرزور indicating the region 'Shahrizor' (a geographic epithet). Word order places the demonstrative and epithet before the name for emphasis.
segments.s515-g15: دگر شهریاران is 'other/another kings/lords' (دگر = 'other'). که introduces a relative/temporal clause 'who/when', and روز نبرد 'the day of battle' specifies the time: 'those other kings, on the day of battle...'. The phrase sets up the temporal circumstance.
segments.s515-g16: سرانشان 'their heads/leaders' is the theme. ز باد ('by/from the wind') indicates cause/agent ('by the wind'). اندر آمد is an archaic intransitive construction 'came into/was carried into' here meaning 'were blown/whirled' (passive-like result). به گرد is an adverbial phrase 'around/about', so the whole formula describes their leaders' heads being whisked around by the wind.
segments.s515-g33: نیابی is a negative present form (نی‑ + یابی) meaning 'you/one will not find/attain'. The prepositional phrase به چون و چرا ('by/through hows and whys') follows it: به = 'by/through', چون و چرا is the idiomatic pair 'how and why' (چون = 'how', چرا = 'why'), and نیز adds the nuance 'also/too' before راه ('way, method'). Word order is verb → instrument phrase (typical in poetry).
segments.s515-g34: The pair نه ... نه is correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. کهتر = 'the inferior/lowly one'. برین here is best read as بر این (on/at this) — بر = 'on/upon' + ین as an orthographic variant of 'این' — so برین دست یابد = 'attains this hand/power' (دست used as 'power/control'). یابد is the present/subjunctive/indicative form 'attains/gets', and شاه = 'king' finishes the correlative 'neither ... nor ...'.
segments.s515-g35: همه نیکوی باید is a general statement: همه = 'all/everyone', نیکوی = 'good/virtuous', باید = 'must/ought (it is necessary)'. The conjunction و links to مردمی which qualifies the following list: مردمی = 'of the people/folk' (i.e. applicable to the people).
segments.s515-g36: This is a coordinated list of desirable things: جوانمردی = 'chivalry / heroic generosity', خوردن here is a noun-like use 'eating/feasting' (not literal imperfective verb), and خرمی = 'mirth/delight'; the Ws are linked by و 'and'.
segments.s515-g37: جز اینت = 'except this' (اینت = a dialectal/poetic form of 'this'); نبینم is first‑person negative 'I do not see' (negated present), and همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('ever/at all' or 'indeed') modifying the negation; بهره‌ای = 'a share/portion'. Together: 'I see no share other than this.'
segments.s515-g38: This is conditional/contrastive: اگر ... وگر ... = 'if ... or if/but if ...'. کهتر آیی = 'you come/arrive in a low condition' (آیی = 'you come'); شهره‌ای = 'you are famed/renowned' (adjectival use). The two alternatives ('if you come lowly or if you are famed') are presented as equally possible circumstances.
segments.s515-g39: Conditional clause: اگر ماند ایدر = 'if ... remains here' (ماند = 'remains/is left', ایدر = 'here'), ز تو = 'from/of you' (z = 'from/because of'), نام زشت = 'an ugly/bad name/reputation of you'. The construction states the condition 'if an evil reputation of yours remains here'.
segments.s515-g40: بدانجا = 'to that place/thereupon', نیایی تو is verb + pronoun with inverted order meaning 'you will not come' (نیایی = negative/imperative/poetic form 'do not come / will not come' here future/negative sense), and خرم بهشت is the place described: 'the joyous/pleasant paradise' — so 'you will not come to that joyous paradise.'
segments.s515-g17: چو here is the subordinating conjunction ‘when’ (archaic/poetic use). The sequence ابری بدی تند is a condensed descriptive clause: ابری (a cloud) + بدی (here as a negative/‘bad’ quality modifying the cloud) + تند (fierce/rapid), and و بارِش تگرگ attaches as ‘and the falling of its hail’ (بار + ـش = its fall/precipitation, تگرگ = hail). Word order is noun + adjectival descriptors, then the coordinated nominal phrase for the hailstorm.
segments.s515-g18: ترا گفتم is a direct clause ‘I told you’ (ترا = you as object + گفتم = I said/told). ایمن شدستی is an archaic/past-form sense of ‘you became/are safe’ (شدستی = you became/you are rendered safe) followed by ز مرگ ‘from death’. The clause therefore reports speech: I told you that you were/are made safe from death.
segments.s515-g19: ز بس begins the causal phrase ‘because of so much/so great a…’ (ز = because of, بس = so much/enough). رزم و پیکار و خون ریختن lists causes: ‘battle and combat and the shedding/spilling of blood’ (ریختن in the verbal noun sense ‘shedding, spilling’). The coordinating و links the nouns.
segments.s515-g20: چه ... چه is the correlative pair meaning ‘whether ... or ...’. Here چه تنها چه با لشکر آویختن means ‘whether (fighting) alone or (clinging/attaching oneself) with an army’; آویختن is used figuratively ‘to attach oneself to, to join/support’ (join with an army).
segments.s515-g21: The clause is reported speech inversion: زمانه ترا داد گفتم جواز = ‘I said (گفتم) that زمانه (Fortune) has given (داد) you (ترا) jواز (permission).’ Word order places the giver (زمانه) first for emphasis; گفتم reports the speaker’s statement.
segments.s515-g22: همی داری is a present/continuative ‘you still have’ (همی = still/indeed, داری = you have). از مردم خویش راز = ‘a secret from your own people’ (از = from, مردم = people, خوی + ش = خویش = one’s own, راز = secret). The phrase states the right/permission referenced above.
segments.s515-g23: چو کردی is ‘when you did/made’ (چو = when, کردی = you did). جهان از بزرگان تهی = ‘the world empty of the great ones’ (جهان = world, از بزرگان = of/from the great, تهی = empty). The clause sets the temporal condition for the following action.
segments.s515-g24: بینداختی is the past 2nd-person ‘you threw/cast away’. تاج شاهنشهی = ‘the crown of kingship/imperial crown’ (تاج = crown; شاهنشهی here denotes the imperial/kingship quality attached to the crown). The verb is a simple past, describing the consequence or action taken.
segments.s515-g1: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that' (ازان = 'from that' + پس = 'after'). بیامد is a past tense verb 'came'. دوان is an adverb/participle meaning 'running/rushing' that modifies بیامد. مادرش = مادر + -ش (third-person possessive) = 'his mother'. Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal phrase + verb phrase + subject with possessive suffix.
segments.s515-g2: فراوان is an adverb 'plentifully/abundantly' modifying the following verb. بمالید is a past tense verb 'rubbed/smeared' (here physical action). رخ is the direct object 'face'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon'. برش appears here as the final element tied to رخ ('his face' or the target of the rubbing) — the line uses an older/poetic surface form; syntactically the clause says she rubbed/smeared his face copiously.
segments.s515-g3: همی is a classical particle here indicating ongoing or habitual speech (rendered 'was saying' / 'kept saying'). گفت is the speech verb 'said' (classical form). کای is the vocative 'O' (an address particle). نامور 'renowned' and پادشا 'king' form the vocative phrase addressing the monarch.
segments.s515-g4: This is a series of epithets joined by و: جهاندار 'sovereign' (a title), نیک‌اختر used as an epithet/name (here treated as a proper epithet), and پارسا 'pious'. The list follows the vocative and completes the address to the king.
segments.s515-g5: This nominal/imperative-like phrase expresses a request/command: به نزدیکی = 'to/into nearness' (come near), اندر تو = 'in you' (classical placement meaning 'be near'), دوری ز من = 'do not be distant from me' (دوری is 'distance; separation', ز marks 'from'). The verb 'be' is implied (typical in Persian imperatives/supplications), so the phrase functions as 'be near; do not stay away from me.'
segments.s515-g6: هم here means 'even' / 'also' emphasizing exceptions are not allowed. از + nouns (دوده 'smoke', لشکر 'army', انجمن 'assembly/crowd') lists the reasons or things for which she asks him not to be distant: 'not even because of smoke, the army, or the crowd.' The sequence is a simple prepositional list introduced by از.
segments.s515-g7: This is an optative/blessing formula. روانم 'my soul' is the subject (روان + -م possessive). روان ترا = 'your soul' (روان + ت = your; را is the object marker placed after the suffix here), followed by بنده 'servant' as predicate noun and باد as the optative particle 'may' (wishing). Word order places the subject, then the object/possessed noun, then the predicate with باد marking the wish: 'May my soul be servant to your soul.'
segments.s515-g8: دل 'heart' is the subject; هرک is an indefinite 'whoever'. زین here functions classically as a particle meaning 'by/for this' (older poetic usage), followed by شاد 'glad/happy' and شد 'became' (past). کنده باد is again optative: کنده 'torn away/uprooted' + باد 'may' = 'may be torn away'. The whole clause is a curse-blessing structure: 'May the heart of whoever is made happy by this be torn away.'

sentences.s515-s29: May the heart of the sovereign of the world be glad; may his body be pure and free from every evil.
sentences.s515-s21: Thus is the custom of the ancient hall: Alexander appeared, and the tale remained here.
sentences.s515-s22: When he slew thirty‑six kings, see what of the world he holds in his fist.
sentences.s515-s23: He founded ten prosperous cities; those cities are now thorny wastelands.
sentences.s515-s24: He sought what no one had ever sought; his speech remained from him across the horizons — and that is enough.
sentences.s515-s25: Better that a poem not be ruined; a poem, like an old house, that endures snow and rain.
sentences.s515-s26: I have passed this Alexandrian barrier; may everything be better, and may Nīk-Akhtar be fortunate.
sentences.s515-s27: Even if time passes somewhat, the written record will remain as a memento of us.
sentences.s515-s28: If you remain a hundred (years) — or a hundred thousand — in the end your affairs will come to dust.
sentences.s515-s13: Like a tree that, when the ship came bearing its load, I see you — consoler of the heart of the earth.
sentences.s515-s14: When the crown of the heavens came down beneath, the great ones were sated with words.
sentences.s515-s15: They hid his chest in the earth; the world has no such fear or dread.
sentences.s515-s16: The wind lifts it up and carries it off in a moment; there was neither apparent justice nor visible oppression.
sentences.s515-s5: After that Roshanak became full of grief; she said thus, "O king, noble man..."
sentences.s515-s6: Where is the sovereign, the possessor Dara? From whom the world used to have its firm support.
sentences.s515-s7: That same Khosrow — and Ashk, and Faryan, and For — that famed Khosrow of Shahrizor.
sentences.s515-s8: Other kings — on the day of battle — their leaders were blown about by the wind.
sentences.s515-s17: You will not find a way by asking hows and whys; neither the lowly nor the king attains this power.
sentences.s515-s18: All must be good, and for the people (there should be) chivalry, feasting, and merriment.
sentences.s515-s19: I see no portion except this; whether you come in low estate or are famed.
sentences.s515-s20: If an ugly (bad) name of yours remains here, you will not come to that joyous paradise.
sentences.s515-s9: When you were like a fierce cloud and its hail-storm fell, I told you, “You are safe from death.”
sentences.s515-s10: Because of so much battle and combat and the shedding of blood — whether alone or with an army to cling to.
sentences.s515-s11: I said that Fortune has given you permission; you still have leave to keep a secret from your own people.
sentences.s515-s12: When you made the world empty of the great ones, you cast away the crown of kingship.
sentences.s515-s1: After that his mother came running and abundantly anointed his face.
sentences.s515-s2: She said: 'O renowned king, sovereign and Nīk-akhter and pious one,'
sentences.s515-s3: Come close; do not be distant from me, not even for smoke, the army, or the assembly.
sentences.s515-s4: May my soul be a servant to your soul; may the heart of whoever is made happy by this be torn away.
